title: The Feature Engineering Roadmap
concept: Feature engineering is a toolkit of distinct skills, each solving a different raw-data problem — cleaning, encoding, scaling, handling outliers, and selecting which features actually help.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is domain knowledge, in the context of feature engineering?

options.text: Real-world understanding of the problem, which guides which features are worth creating
Knowledge of a specific programming language
The total row count of a raw dataset

exercises.explain: Right! You now know WHY feature engineering matters and WHAT guides good features. Today: a map of HOW you'll actually build them.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model's quality is capped by the quality of its features, no matter how advanced the algorithm.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Since features matter this much, it's worth previewing exactly which feature-engineering skills you're about to pick up.

cards.title: You know why. Here's how.
cards.body: You've learned why feature engineering matters, why bad features cap a model, and why domain knowledge guides good ones. Now it's time to open the toolbox and see exactly which tools you're about to pick up.

cards.title: What is the feature engineering roadmap?
cards.body: The roadmap is the sequence of hands-on feature engineering skills coming next, each one built to solve a different kind of raw-data problem.

cards.title: A construction crew's toolbox
cards.body: A construction crew doesn't use a hammer for every job — they reach for a drill, a level, or a saw depending on the problem in front of them. Feature engineering works the same way: each raw-data problem gets its own tool.

cards.title: Five tools, five problems
cards.body: Cleaning fixes messy or missing values. Encoding turns categories like "red" or "blue" into a form a model can use. Scaling puts numbers on comparable ranges. Outliers handles extreme, unusual values. Selection decides which features actually earn their place.

cards.title: It's not one single step
cards.body: It's easy to picture feature engineering as one action you do once. In reality, it's a whole sequence of distinct skills, each tackling a different issue — you'll build each one, one lesson at a time.

cards.title: Before the toolkit vs. after
cards.body: What's ahead over the next five units:

left.text:
You know WHY features matter
and WHAT to aim for

exercises.prompt: Which of these correctly names one of the five upcoming feature engineering toolkit topics?

options.text: Encoding: turning categories into a form a model can use
Photosynthesis: how plants convert sunlight into energy
Rendering: drawing pixels onto a screen
Compiling: converting code into machine instructions

exercises.explain: Exactly! Encoding — turning categories like "red" or "blue" into model-usable form — is one of the five upcoming topics.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Feature engineering is best thought of as a single, one-time action rather than a sequence of distinct skills.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! It's a toolkit of separate skills — cleaning, encoding, scaling, outliers, and selection — each solving a different problem.

exercises.sentence: ___ handles extreme, unusual values that could otherwise distort a model.
exercises.explain: Right! Handling outliers is one of the five toolkit topics coming up.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is one of the five feature engineering toolkit topics previewed today?

options.text: Selection: deciding which features actually earn their place in the model
Refrigeration: keeping perishable food cold during shipping
Compression: shrinking a video file's storage size

exercises.explain: Correct! Selection — deciding which features actually help — is one of the five upcoming skills.

exercises.prompt: Why does the feature engineering roadmap use a construction toolbox analogy?

options.text: Because each raw-data problem gets its own specific tool, just like a crew reaches for different tools for different jobs
Because feature engineering is only used in the construction industry
Because all five toolkit topics are actually the exact same technique
Because a hammer can solve every raw-data problem on its own

exercises.explain: Right! Just like a hammer isn't used for every job, cleaning, encoding, scaling, outliers, and selection each solve a different kind of raw-data problem.

exercises.prompt: A learner says: "I already know why feature engineering matters, so I can just skip straight to picking an algorithm." Based on today's roadmap, what's missing from that plan?

options.text: The actual hands-on toolkit — cleaning, encoding, scaling, outliers, and selection — that turns raw data into usable features
Nothing — understanding why is the only thing that's ever needed
A faster computer, which replaces the need for any of the five toolkit topics
More raw data, which automatically performs all five toolkit steps by itself

exercises.explain: Exactly! Knowing WHY matters, but the five upcoming skills are what actually let you DO feature engineering on real data.

exercises.prompt: Put the five upcoming toolkit topics in the order previewed today: cleaning, encoding, scaling, outliers, selection. Which option lists them in that same order?

options.text: Cleaning → Encoding → Scaling → Outliers → Selection
Selection → Outliers → Scaling → Encoding → Cleaning
Encoding → Cleaning → Selection → Scaling → Outliers
Scaling → Selection → Cleaning → Outliers → Encoding

exercises.explain: Right! Cleaning → Encoding → Scaling → Outliers → Selection is the exact toolkit sequence ahead. Time to start building it, one tool at a time!
